vulnerabilities.title: LendingTerm._partialRepay minBorrow Check Uses Inconsistent Units After creditMultiplier Decrease
vulnerabilities.summary: In _partialRepay(), the remaining borrowAmount after partial repay is compared against `ProfitManager.minBorrow()`. However, borrowAmount is stored as the original CREDIT amount at borrow time, while minBorrow() returns `_minBorrow * 1e18 / creditMultiplier`. When creditMultiplier decreases (due to bad debt), minBorrow() increases, potentially exceeding the stored borrowAmount even though the loan's effective debt in peg terms is still well above the minimum. This blocks partial repayments entir

description.desc: require(borrowAmount - issuanceDecrease > ProfitManager.minBorrow()) compares stored nominal amount against dynamically scaled minimum
Affected function: minBorrow()

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. After any creditMultiplier decrease, all existing loans with borrowAmount < (_minBorrow * 1e18 / newCreditMultiplier) lose the ability to do partial repayments. For terms with maxDelayBetweenPartialRepay > 0, these loans could become callable (via partialRepayDelayPassed) because the borrower cannot perform the required partial repayment. This forces borrowers into either full repayment or liquidation unnecessarily. The governor can mitigate by lowering _minBorrow, but this is a reactive fix. The number of affected loans scales with the severity of the creditMultiplier decrease.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree Infinite Loop When User Has Zero-Weight Gauge
vulnerabilities.summary: In ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree(), the loop counter `i` is only incremented inside the `if (userGaugeWeight != 0)` block. If a user has a gauge with 0 weight in their gauge set (added via incrementGauge(gauge, 0)), and the loop reaches that gauge before freeing enough weight, it will loop infinitely on the zero-weight entry, causing an out-of-gas revert. This blocks all token transfers and burns for the affected user.

description.desc: unchecked { ++i; } only inside if (userGaugeWeight != 0) block; zero-weight gauge causes infinite iteration

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect causes a self-inflicted DoS where affected users cannot transfer or burn their GUILD tokens until they manually remove the 0-weight gauge via decrementGauge(gauge, 0). This could also affect the SurplusGuildMinter contract if mintRatio is set to 0 by governance, as stake() would call incrementGauge with 0 weight. Recovery is possible but requires awareness of the issue.

vulnerabilities.title: ProfitManager.notifyPnL Reverts on Large Loss Due to Underflow in creditMultiplier Calculation
vulnerabilities.summary: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL(), during loss handling, after depleting the surplus buffer, the remaining loss is used to update creditMultiplier: `newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply`. If the remaining loss exceeds creditTotalSupply, the subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8.13 (checked arithmetic), causing a revert. This means the loss can never be processed, blocking the auction settlement and locking collateral permanently.

description.desc: uint256 newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply; underflows when loss > creditTotalSupply

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. When this state is reached: (1) The affected loan's collateral is permanently locked in the LendingTerm. (2) nAuctionsInProgress in AuctionHouse cannot be decremented, blocking setAuctionHouse. (3) The system's creditMultiplier should be lowered but cannot be, meaning all other CREDIT holders maintain an inflated value. (4) Recovery requires governor emergencyAction to manually manipulate ProfitManager state (set creditMultiplier to a lower value) and LendingTerm state (close the loan). This is a system-bricking scenario under cascading loss conditions.

vulnerabilities.title: PSM decimalCorrection may underflow / revert for peg tokens with >18 decimals, blocking PSM deployment or bricking mint/redeem paths
vulnerabilities.summary: SimplePSM computes `decimalCorrection = 10 ** (18 - decimals)` using `ERC20(_pegToken).decimals()`. If the configured peg token reports decimals > 18 (or a malicious token returns an unexpected value), the subtraction underflows and deployment reverts, or the PSM becomes unusable. This is a concrete code defect: no bounds check on decimals.

description.desc: decimalCorrection = 10 ** (18 - decimals); subtraction underflows if decimals>18

vulnerabilities.impact: The direct impact is denial of service at deployment time — SimplePSM cannot be deployed for tokens with >18 decimals. The indirect impact depends on protocol architecture: if only one PSM is expected per market, and the intended peg token has >18 decimals, the entire peg maintenance mechanism for that market would be unavailable. The severity is reduced by the fact that (1) most stablecoins have 6 or 18 decimals, (2) the failure occurs at deploy time not runtime, and (3) the deployer would see the revert immediately. However, the code is objectively wrong in not validating its input.

vulnerabilities.title: Loan ID generation uses timestamp-only entropy causing same-second self-DoS and potential cross-user collision scenarios under account abstraction batching
vulnerabilities.summary: LendingTerm._borrow derives `loanId = keccak256(abi.encode(borrower, address(this), block.timestamp))` and permanently prevents re-use because `borrowTime` stays non-zero after close. This makes it impossible for the same borrower to open two loans in the same second on the same term (even if the first is repaid later), and can cause user-facing DoS for smart wallets/batch executors that submit multiple borrows within the same timestamp.

description.desc: loanId = keccak256(abi.encode(borrower,address(this),block.timestamp)); require(loans[loanId].borrowTime == 0)

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect prevents same-borrower, same-term, same-second multiple borrows. Impact: (1) Smart wallets/batchers cannot open multiple positions atomically. (2) Automated strategies that rely on batched borrows will fail. (3) On L2s with coarse timestamps, even separate transactions may collide. No direct fund loss occurs, but the DoS can break integrator workflows and prevent legitimate protocol usage patterns. The severity is appropriately classified as MEDIUM given the DoS nature without direct financial impact.

vulnerabilities.title: SurplusGuildMinter getRewards() Checks Slashed Flag Against Uninitialized Memory, Enabling Permissionless Theft of All Staked CREDIT
vulnerabilities.summary: In SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards(), the `slashed` condition is evaluated against the default-initialized (zero) `userStake` return variable BEFORE the actual user stake is loaded from storage. Since `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is 0 in the uninitialized struct, any term where `GuildToken.lastGaugeLoss(term) > 0` will cause `slashed = true` for ALL stakers, regardless of when they staked. This zeroes the user's stake record, permanently locking their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer and orphaning t

description.desc: slashed flag evaluated against default-zero userStake.lastGaugeLoss before storage load at line 228: `userStake = _stakes[user][term]`

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Every SurplusGuildMinter staker on any term that has experienced a gauge loss (even historically) is vulnerable. An attacker can call getRewards(victim, term) for each victim to permanently destroy their stake. The victim loses 100% of their staked CREDIT (locked in term surplus buffer with no recovery path) and their GUILD gauge weight becomes orphaned. The attack cost is just gas. This is a permissionless griefing attack with total loss for victims.

vulnerabilities.title: Stale canOffboard Flag Allows Immediate Re-Offboarding After Re-Onboarding, Permanently Pausing PSM Redemptions
vulnerabilities.summary: When a lending term is offboarded, `canOffboard[term]` is set to true and `nOffboardingsInProgress` is incremented. If the term is re-onboarded by governance (via addGauge) before cleanup() is called, `canOffboard[term]` persists as true. An attacker can immediately call `offboard(term)` again without any new poll or quorum, bypassing the democratic offboarding process. This double-increments `nOffboardingsInProgress` but cleanup() can only decrement it once (setting `canOffboard[term] = false`)

description.desc: offboard() only checks canOffboard[term] which persists from previous offboarding cycle; no check that the term is currently in an active offboarding round
Affected function: redeem

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - The attack permanently corrupts the nOffboardingsInProgress counter, preventing PSM redemptions from being unpaused through the normal cleanup() mechanism. This effectively creates a permanent denial of service on CREDIT redemptions via the PSM, impacting all CREDIT holders. While governance can manually call SimplePSM.setRedemptionsPaused(false), this requires active governance intervention and the counter remains corrupted, meaning any future legitimate offboarding/cleanup cycle will also fail to properly unpause. Each repetition of the attack adds +1 to the permanent counter inflation.

vulnerabilities.title: GuildVetoGovernor Deterministic ProposalId Prevents Repeated Veto Attempts on Same Timelock Action
vulnerabilities.summary: GuildVetoGovernor.createVeto() generates a deterministic proposalId from the timelockId (via the OZ Governor's propose mechanism hashing targets/values/calldatas/description). Since all these parameters are deterministic for a given timelockId, the same proposalId is generated each time. If a veto proposal fails to reach quorum (state = Defeated), OZ Governor prevents creating a new proposal with the same proposalId. An attacker can create a veto proposal early when few votes are available, ensu

description.desc: createVeto builds deterministic proposal parameters from timelockId; OZ Governor prevents duplicate proposalIds; once Defeated, no retry possible

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - The veto mechanism is a critical security feature designed to protect against malicious timelock actions. If an attacker can neutralize the veto with a single gas-only transaction, the honest minority guarantee is undermined. Any governance action that passes quorum (even by a slim margin or through collusion) becomes unvettoable if the attacker front-runs the veto creation. The impact extends to the entire protocol since timelock actions can modify critical parameters, grant roles, or extract value.

vulnerabilities.title: ProfitSharingConfig Precision Loss from uint32 Truncation Causes Systematic Profit Misdistribution
vulnerabilities.summary: ProfitManager.setProfitSharingConfig() validates that splits sum to 1e18 at 18-decimal precision, then stores them as uint32 values after dividing by 1e9. This truncation loses up to (1e9 - 1) per split. When splits are read back via getProfitSharingConfig(), they're multiplied by 1e9, and creditSplit is computed as the residual (1e18 - surplusBufferSplit - guildSplit - otherSplit). The truncation means the actual distribution differs from configured intent, with the residual creditSplit absorbi

description.desc: uint32(surplusBufferSplit / 1e9) truncates; configured splits summing to 1e18 may not reconstruct accurately after storage round-trip

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact per distribution is at most ~3 gwei (3e9 wei) of misdistribution, which is economically negligible. However, this constitutes a genuine code defect: the code validates splits at 18-decimal precision but stores at 9-decimal precision without requiring inputs to be 1e9-aligned or documenting the truncation. The credit holders systematically receive slightly more than their configured share at the expense of surplus buffer, guild holders, and/or other recipient. The discrepancy between configured and actual splits could mislead governance.

vulnerabilities.title: creditMultiplier Approaching Zero Bricks Multiple System Functions via Division by Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: creditMultiplier has no floor protection and can reach extremely low values or theoretically 0 through cascading losses. If creditMultiplier approaches 0: (1) getLoanDebt divides by creditMultiplier, amplifying all debts toward infinity; (2) SimplePSM.getMintAmountOut divides by creditMultiplier, allowing infinite CREDIT minting for minimal peg tokens; (3) minBorrow() divides by creditMultiplier, potentially overflowing; (4) the multiplier update formula itself can reach 0 if loss exactly equals

description.desc: newCreditMultiplier has no floor; if it reaches 0, division by creditMultiplier in getLoanDebt (LendingTerm.sol:243) and getMintAmountOut (SimplePSM.sol:92) causes revert
Affected function: getLoanDebt
Affected function: getMintAmountOut

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. If creditMultiplier reaches 0: all loans become unqueryable (getLoanDebt reverts), no new minting from PSM possible (getMintAmountOut reverts), no new borrows possible (minBorrow reverts), existing loans cannot be repaid (getLoanDebt used in repay), auctions cannot function. The entire protocol is permanently bricked with no recovery path except governance emergency actions. Even near-zero values (before reaching 0) allow massive CREDIT minting from PSM for negligible peg tokens, effectively draining PSM reserves.

vulnerabilities.title: Perpetual Yield Delay via Micro-Distributions in Rebase Distributor
vulnerabilities.summary: The `ERC20RebaseDistributor.distribute()` function resets the 30-day interpolation period (`DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD`) for ALL pending unminted rewards every time it is called. An attacker can repeatedly call `distribute(1 wei)` every few hours. Each call takes the current unminted rewards and stretches them over a new 30-day period, perpetually flattening the distribution curve and preventing rebasing CREDIT holders from ever receiving their full accumulated yield.

description.desc: targetTimestamp is unconditionally set to block.timestamp + DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD on every distribute call

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a significant delay (not complete prevention) of yield distribution to all rebasing CREDIT holders. With sustained hourly micro-distributions, the effective delivery of rewards follows an exponential decay curve instead of linear interpolation, delivering only ~63% of expected yield after 30 days instead of 100%. This is a griefing attack that degrades protocol economics. While the share price still monotonically increases (INV-ECON-005 is technically not violated), the rate of increase is severely diminished. The hypothesis slightly overstates the impact as 'near 100% reduction' - the actual reduction depends on attack frequency but is approximately 37% at the 30-day mark with hourly calls.

vulnerabilities.title: PSM Peg Denial of Service via Strategic Term Offboarding
vulnerabilities.summary: `LendingTermOffboarding.offboard()` pauses `SimplePSM` redemptions. Redemptions are only unpaused in `cleanup()`, which requires `LendingTerm.issuance() == 0`. An attacker with enough GUILD to reach the offboarding quorum can pass an offboard poll for any active term, immediately pausing the PSM. Because `cleanup()` requires all loans to be fully called and auctioned (which takes time and depends on market liquidity), the PSM peg mechanism is completely disabled for an extended period, breaking

description.desc: SimplePSM(psm).setRedemptionsPaused(true) called on offboard, unpaused only when issuance == 0
Affected function: setRedemptionsPaused

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - The PSM is the primary mechanism for maintaining CREDIT token peg. Disabling it for days or weeks can cause CREDIT to lose its peg on secondary markets. All CREDIT holders are affected. The attack can be repeated against different terms. Additionally, the nOffboardingsInProgress counter issue (INV-STATE-004) can make this permanent if a term is re-onboarded and re-offboarded. While governance can theoretically intervene via the timelock to unpause PSM directly, this adds delay and may not be fast enough to prevent peg deviation.

vulnerabilities.title: Whitelisted Contract Gas Limit DoS via Unlimited Delegates
vulnerabilities.summary: `ERC20MultiVotes` allows governance to whitelist contracts to bypass `maxDelegates` via `setContractExceedMaxDelegates`. If a whitelisted contract accumulates a massive number of delegates, any attempt to transfer or burn its tokens will trigger `_decrementVotesUntilFree()`, which loops over all delegates. This loop will exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking the contract's tokens.

description.desc: Unbounded loop over delegateList in _decrementVotesUntilFree

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. Impact is permanent locking of all tokens held by the whitelisted contract. This could include significant TVL if the contract is a yield aggregator or protocol integration. The vulnerability requires governance whitelisting as a precondition, but once whitelisted, there is no safeguard against accumulating too many delegates. The protocol's own SECURITY NOTE acknowledges maxDelegates as critical for gas DOS prevention, yet the bypass mechanism removes this protection entirely.

vulnerabilities.title: Debt Ceiling Check in _decrementGaugeWeight Prevents Slashing of GUILD Voters on Fully-Utilized Terms
vulnerabilities.summary: GuildToken._decrementGaugeWeight() requires that after decrementing weight, the term's issuance does not exceed the new debt ceiling: `require(issuance <= debtCeilingAfterDecrement)`. This check also applies during applyGaugeLoss(), which calls _decrementGaugeWeight to remove the voter's weight. If a term's issuance is at or near its debt ceiling, reducing gauge weight would drop the ceiling below issuance, causing the require to revert. This prevents slashing of GUILD voters who should be penal

description.desc: _decrementGaugeWeight checks LendingTerm(gauge).debtCeiling(-int256(weight)) against issuance; applyGaugeLoss calls _decrementGaugeWeight; if issuance >= debtCeilingAfterDecrement, revert blocks slashing

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: The core slashing mechanism of the protocol (applyGaugeLoss) can be blocked for any term with high utilization. This defeats the economic incentive alignment where GUILD voters bear loss for backing risky terms. Specifically: (1) Voters on high-utilization terms are protected from slashing until issuance decreases; (2) If the gauge is deprecated and is the only gauge of its type, division by zero permanently blocks slashing; (3) Users cannot transfer or burn their GUILD tokens allocated to the affected gauge (since transfer/transferFrom also call _decrementWeightUntilFree → _decrementGaugeWeight); (4) This creates moral hazard - voters can support risky terms knowing they can avoid slashing during high utilization periods.

vulnerabilities.title: Strict minBorrow Check Prevents Exact Partial Repayments
vulnerabilities.summary: The `_partialRepay` function uses a strict greater-than (`>`) check against `minBorrow`, preventing borrowers from reducing their loan balance to exactly the minimum allowed amount.

description.desc: Strict inequality used where inclusive inequality is expected

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM

vulnerabilities.title: Governor forgive() Breaks Active Auctions
vulnerabilities.summary: If a governor calls `forgive()` on a loan that is currently in an active auction, the loan is closed but the auction remains open permanently, bricking the AuctionHouse migration process.

description.desc: State transition bypassing required cleanup

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. This is a governor-triggered defect (requires GOVERNOR role), not an externally exploitable vulnerability. However, it represents a genuine code defect: a missing state check that creates an irrecoverable inconsistency between LendingTerm and AuctionHouse state. The practical impact is inability to migrate AuctionHouse for any LendingTerm that had forgive() called on an active auction. Recovery via emergencyAction is possible but requires manual storage manipulation.

vulnerabilities.title: Division by Zero in debtCeiling Locks GUILD Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: If all gauges of a specific type are deprecated, `totalTypeWeight` becomes 0. Any call to `debtCeiling` for that type will revert due to division by zero, breaking `_decrementGaugeWeight` and locking users' GUILD tokens.

description.desc: uint256 debtCeilingBefore = (totalBorrowedCredit * toleratedGaugeWeight) / totalWeight; where totalWeight can be 0

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity DoS. Users' GUILD tokens allocated to the deprecated gauge are locked until all loans are settled. Since the term is deprecated, loans can be called immediately, and auctions can proceed. The lock duration depends on auction timing (configured midPoint and auctionDuration). While not permanent, it prevents GUILD voters from reallocating their voting power to other gauges during this period, reducing protocol governance flexibility. The division by zero also affects applyGaugeLoss for the same gauge if there's been a loss notification. In the worst case with very long auction durations or stuck collateral, the lock could persist for an extended period.

vulnerabilities.title: SimplePSM can burn user CREDIT for zero pegToken output due to rounding, enabling griefing via tiny redemptions and confusing accounting during stress
vulnerabilities.summary: SimplePSM.getRedeemAmountOut computes `amountOut = (amountIn * creditMultiplier) / 1e18 / decimalCorrection`. For small amountIn or low creditMultiplier, amountOut can round down to 0, yet redeem() still burns `amountIn` CREDIT and decrements pegTokenBalance by 0, effectively allowing users (or bots) to create on-chain noise and reduce their own CREDIT to 0 output. While self-harm, this can be used to spam 'redeem' events and complicate operational monitoring during incidents, and may be leverag

description.desc: amountOut = (amountIn * creditMultiplier) / 1e18 / decimalCorrection; redeem burns amountIn even if amountOut==0

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity as a code defect. The amounts that trigger zero output are very small under normal conditions (sub-1e12 CREDIT for USDC). However: (1) it violates the invariant that non-zero input should produce non-zero output, (2) as creditMultiplier drops, the threshold for zero output increases, (3) integrations that don't check amountOut could experience silent value destruction, (4) events emitted with amountOut=0 can confuse monitoring systems.

vulnerabilities.title: ProfitManager Guild Profit Permanently Stuck When Gauge Weight Is Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL(), when profit is distributed, amountForGuild is calculated and subtracted from the total to compute amountForCredit. However, if the gauge's weight is 0 at the time of profit notification, the gaugeProfitIndex is not updated, and the CREDIT tokens allocated to GUILD stakers remain stuck in ProfitManager with no claimant. This can occur when a term is offboarded (gauge deprecated, weight removed from totals) but auctions from that term still settle with profit.

description.desc: if (_gaugeWeight != 0) { update profit index } -- amountForGuild CREDIT stays in ProfitManager when weight is 0

vulnerabilities.impact: Financial impact: CREDIT tokens proportional to guildSplit * total_profits from 0-weight gauges are permanently locked. This reduces the effective profit distribution to all other stakeholders (credit holders lose amountForCredit that was reduced by amountForGuild). Recovery is possible via governor's emergencyAction on ProfitManager, but requires governance intervention. The invariant INV-STATE-007 (surplus_buffer_solvency: ProfitManager CREDIT balance >= surplusBuffer + termSurplusBuffer + unclaimed guild rewards) may appear satisfied because the orphaned tokens contribute to the CREDIT balance but are not tracked by any accounting variable.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20RebaseDistributor updateTotalRebasingShares Can Overflow uint224 Trapping Last Rebasers
vulnerabilities.summary: In ERC20RebaseDistributor.updateTotalRebasingShares(), when shares decrease significantly (most rebasers exit), the adjusted target share price `targetNewSharePrice = currentRebasingSharePrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange` can overflow uint224 if percentChange is very small. This happens because dividing by a tiny percentChange amplifies the delta. SafeCastLib.safeCastTo224 would revert, blocking the remaining rebaser from exiting rebase.

description.desc: targetNewSharePrice = currentPrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange; percentChange can be very small, amplifying result beyond uint224

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH

vulnerabilities.title: ProfitManager.initializeReferences is a one-time assert-based initializer; mis-ordering or partial setup can permanently brick core accounting
vulnerabilities.summary: ProfitManager.initializeReferences uses `assert(credit==0 && guild==0 && psm==0)` (not `require`) and sets critical addresses. If called with wrong addresses, or if one of those references is accidentally set in a prior transaction (even to a non-zero incorrect value), the contract becomes permanently misconfigured without an in-code recovery path. Because it uses `assert`, failures consume all gas and indicate an invariant violation, complicating ops and monitoring.

description.desc: assert(credit==0 && guild==0 && psm==0); sets core references with no later setter

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact if triggered: ProfitManager is the central accounting contract controlling creditMultiplier, surplus buffers, profit distribution, and loss socialization. Misconfiguration permanently breaks: (1) all borrowing (debtCeiling depends on totalBorrowedCredit), (2) all repayments/auctions (notifyPnL would fail), (3) all profit claims (claimGaugeRewards), (4) PSM operations (redeemableCredit). LOW likelihood: requires GOVERNOR error during a one-time initialization step.

vulnerabilities.title: Profit Index Dust Accumulation Permanently Leaks CREDIT
vulnerabilities.summary: In `ProfitManager.notifyPnL()`, the `gaugeProfitIndex` is incremented by `(amountForGuild * 1e18) / _gaugeWeight`. Because this division truncates the remainder, a small amount of CREDIT is left unaccounted for in the index. Over thousands of loan repayments across many terms, this truncated 'dust' accumulates in the `ProfitManager` contract. Since there is no sweep or recovery function for this dust, the CREDIT tokens are permanently leaked from the circulating supply.

description.desc: gaugeProfitIndex[gauge] = _gaugeProfitIndex + (amountForGuild * 1e18) / _gaugeWeight; (truncation)

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is negligible. Even under extreme conditions (10,000 repayments with a gauge weight of 1e24), the total accumulated dust would be approximately 1e-8 CREDIT. This does not meaningfully affect protocol solvency, user balances, or CREDIT supply. The surplus_buffer_solvency invariant (INV-ECON-002) is not violated because the dust is a rounding remainder, not a solvency issue.

vulnerabilities.title: Auction callback trust + lack of reentrancy guards may allow malicious collateral tokens to reenter and corrupt loan/issuance via external calls during onBid/repay paths
vulnerabilities.summary: LendingTerm interacts with arbitrary collateral tokens via SafeERC20.safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom in _borrow/_addCollateral/_repay/onBid. There are no reentrancy guards. While the protocol documentation says rebasing/fee-on-transfer collateral is excluded, a malicious ERC20 collateral (or an upgradeable collateral token later upgraded) could execute callbacks (e.g., ERC777-style hooks or crafted ERC20 with external calls) during transfer to reenter into LendingTerm methods (borrow/addCollateral

description.desc: External token transfers (collateralToken.safeTransferFrom / safeTransfer) without nonReentrant; multiple external protocol calls in same flow

Affected function: createTerm(address,(address collateralToken,uint256 maxDebtPerCollateralToken,uint256 interestRate,uint256 maxDelayBetweenPartialRepay,uint256 minPartialRepayPercent,uint256 openingFee,uint256 hardCap))

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH

vulnerabilities.title: AuctionHouse.forgive Forces Total Loss on Valuable Collateral When Auction Expires Without Bids
vulnerabilities.summary: When an auction reaches its full duration without any bids, the bid() function reverts (require creditAsked != 0 fails) and only forgive() can close the auction. forgive() calls onBid with collateralToBorrower=0, collateralToBidder=0, creditFromBidder=0, marking the loan as a total loss. The collateral remains permanently locked in the LendingTerm. Even if the collateral is valuable and the borrower is willing to repay, the auction forces a total loss once the duration expires.

description.desc: At auctionDuration: collateralReceived = full, creditAsked = 0; bid() requires creditAsked != 0 so reverts; only forgive() works sending 0 collateral to both parties

vulnerabilities.impact: The described behavior is by design. When forgive() is triggered: (1) The loss is reported to ProfitManager, potentially affecting creditMultiplier - this is correct since the debt is unrecoverable if collateral is frozen. (2) Collateral stays in LendingTerm - governance can recover it via emergencyAction when/if it becomes transferable. (3) GUILD voters are slashed - correct since they voted for a term that produced bad debt. The impact is appropriate for the scenario the code is designed to handle.
